Guide · ROI Calculator

Construction Software ROI Calculator (2026)

Before you spend $500/month, here's how to calculate what you'll actually get back — with real numbers for your business.

✓ Updated April 2026

Construction software at $500–$1,000/month sounds expensive until you calculate what you're currently leaving on the table. This calculator-style guide walks through the five areas where software creates measurable ROI — with formulas you can apply to your specific numbers.

How to use this guide

For each category, fill in your numbers from the questions. At the end, sum your monthly value estimates and compare against the software cost. If the math doesn't work, it's either the wrong software or you're not using it to full potential.

Category 1: Administrative time savings

The calculation

Estimate how many hours per week your team currently spends on administrative tasks that software would automate or streamline:

  • Creating and distributing schedules
  • Chasing status updates from field teams
  • Searching for documents, photos, and drawings
  • Creating and formatting invoices
  • Following up on unpaid invoices
  • Compiling reports for owners or stakeholders

Your inputs:

  • Hours saved per week: ___
  • Fully-loaded hourly cost of the person doing this work: $___
  • Weekly value: Hours × Rate = $___
  • Monthly value: Weekly value × 4.3 = $___

Industry benchmark: The average contractor using manual/spreadsheet processes spends 8–15 hours/week per project on administrative work that software reduces to 3–5 hours. On 3 active projects, that's 15–36 hours saved weekly.

Example calculation: Save 10 hours/week at $55/hour fully loaded = $550/week = $2,365/month

Category 2: Change order capture improvement

The calculation

This is where most contractors leave the most money. Research consistently shows that 20–35% of extra work performed is never billed, because it wasn't documented, wasn't presented formally, or the conversation was avoided.

Your inputs:

  • Annual revenue: $___
  • Estimated percentage of work currently unbilled due to missing change orders: ___%
  • Current annual unbilled work: Revenue × unbilled % = $___
  • Realistic improvement with software (capture 50% of currently missed COs): $___
  • Monthly value: Annual improvement ÷ 12 = $___

Industry benchmark: Remodelers and custom builders typically have change order rates of 15–40% of original contract value. If 25% of those COs aren't being captured, a contractor doing $2M/year is leaving $75,000–$150,000 on the table annually.

Example calculation: $2M revenue × 5% missed COs = $100K/year. Capturing 60% with software = $60K/year = $5,000/month

Buildertrend's change order workflow makes it easy to present, approve, and invoice change orders through the client portal — from any device, in minutes.

Try Buildertrend →

Category 3: Payment acceleration

The calculation

Faster invoicing and easier payment methods accelerate cash collection. Industry data shows contractors using digital invoicing and online payment options collect 8–15 days faster than those using paper invoices and checks.

Your inputs:

  • Average monthly accounts receivable balance: $___
  • Days currently to collect after invoice: ___
  • Expected improvement with digital invoicing/payment: 10–15 days faster
  • Your cost of capital (interest rate on line of credit or opportunity cost): ___%
  • Monthly value: (A/R balance × days faster / 365) × cost of capital = $___

Industry benchmark: A contractor with $300,000 in A/R collecting 12 days faster at 8% cost of capital saves approximately $790/month. Larger A/R balances amplify this significantly.

Additional payment benefit: Fewer bounced checks, credit card payment capability, and auto-reminders reduce payment disputes and collection effort.

Category 4: Estimating accuracy and win rate

The calculation

Better estimating software improves both accuracy (fewer underpriced jobs) and win rate (faster turnaround on bids):

Accuracy improvement:

  • How many jobs per year do you lose money on due to estimating errors? ___
  • Average loss per underpriced job: $___
  • Expected reduction in estimating errors with better software: 50%
  • Annual value: (number of bad jobs × 50%) × average loss = $___

Win rate improvement:

  • Current bid-to-win ratio: ___ out of ___ bids
  • If faster turnaround or better presentation wins 1 additional job per quarter:
  • Average gross profit per job: $___
  • Additional annual gross profit: 4 jobs × average GP = $___

Category 5: Dispute and rework cost reduction

The calculation

Construction disputes — over scope, schedule, extras, and quality — are expensive even when you "win." Legal fees, management time, relationship damage, and delayed final payments all cost money. Good documentation reduces disputes by making the facts clear before positions harden.

Your inputs:

  • Number of significant disputes per year: ___
  • Average cost per dispute (management time + legal + delayed payment): $___
  • Expected reduction in disputes with better documentation: 40–60%
  • Annual value: (disputes × reduction %) × average cost = $___

Industry benchmark: A contractor with 3 disputes per year at $5,000 each (modest estimate for management time and delayed payment) saves $6,000–$9,000/year by reducing disputes by 40–60%. Larger disputes can be much more significant.

Your total ROI calculation

Category Your Monthly Value
Administrative time savings $___
Change order capture improvement $___
Payment acceleration $___
Estimating accuracy / win rate $___
Dispute cost reduction $___
Total monthly value $___
Software cost $499–999/mo
Net monthly ROI $___

What this calculator typically shows

For a residential contractor doing $1.5–3M annually with 3–5 active projects, this calculation typically produces:

  • Administrative savings: $1,500–$3,000/month
  • Change order improvement: $2,000–$6,000/month
  • Payment acceleration: $300–$800/month
  • Estimating improvement: $500–$1,500/month
  • Dispute reduction: $200–$600/month
  • Total: $4,500–$11,900/month

Against a Buildertrend cost of $499/month, that's a 9–24x ROI. Even at conservative estimates that cut these numbers in half, the math works comfortably.

The caveat: software only pays when you use it

The ROI calculation above assumes you actually implement the platform, train your team, and change your workflows. Software that sits unused because adoption failed returns zero on your investment. The biggest ROI predictor isn't which software you choose — it's whether you commit to the implementation process.

Buildertrend — the most widely used residential construction platform. Start free, see the features, and run your own numbers.
